coredata.dc:title: Seeking brightness from nature: Sustainable carbon dots-based AIEgens with tunable emission wavelength from natural rosin
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Min
Han, Youqi
Ni, Jiaxin
Li, Yudong
Han, Shiyan
Li, Shujun
Yu, Haipeng
Zhang, Chunlei
Liu, Shouxin
Li, Jian
Chen, Zhijun
coredata.dc:description: Aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) have many applications, including bioimaging, organic light-emitting diodes, sensing and anti-cancer therapy. Most AIEgens, however, suffer from unsustainable synthesis. Here, we have developed carbon dots-based AIEgens (CD-AIEgens), with adjustable fluorescence wavelength, that can be prepared from sustainable rosin in a one-step hydrothermal process. The fluorescence wavelength of solutions containing as-prepared CD-AIEgens can be adjusted between 430 nm and 582 nm by varying the concentration. The adjustable emissive wavelength is attributed to inherent FRET between the carbon cores and aggregates of tricyclic phenanthrene moieties on the surface of the CD-AIEgens. Making use of their adjustable fluorescence, we successfully used the CD-AIEgens as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink for encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rosin
AIE
Carbon dots
Adjustable fluorescence wavelength

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) High resolution TEM image of CD-AIEgens (inset: lattice fringe images); b) Size distribution of CD-AIEgens; c) Full XPS spectrum of CD-AIEgens; d) FT-IR spectrum of CD-AIEgens.

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-AIEgens in mixtures of ethanol and water (Ex = 365 nm); b) Fluorescence decay curves of ethanol solutions CD-AIEgens with water fractions of 0% and 70%; c) Intensity of CD-AIEgen fluorescence on repeated cycling between room temperature (RT) and 70 °C; d) Fluorescence emission spectra of ethanol solutions CD-AIEgens with glycerol fractions of 0%–70% (Ex = 365 nm).

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of ethanol solutions of CD-AIEgens with different concentrations(0.01–0.6 mg mL−1) (Ex = 365 nm); b) Normalized spectra of ethanol solutions of CD-AIEgens with different concentrations (0.6–35 mg mL−1) (Ex = 365 nm); c) CIE chromaticity diagram of CD-AIEgens with different concentrations (0.01–35 mg mL−1) (inset: color trend chart of solutions with different concentrations); d) Photographs of silica composite gels containing different amounts of CD-AIEgens under daylight and UV light; e) Schematic showing fluorescence resonance energy transfer between TPM aggregates and carbon cores.

a) Printing and washing experiments with CD-AIEgens; b) Images printed on paper with CD-AIEgens, illuminated with UV light (365 nm), after air drying (left) and adding ethanol (right); c) Schematic showing mechanism of CD-AIEgens as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink.
Schematic illustration of CD-AIEgens and their tunable fluorescence (Rosin is mainly composed of resin acid. For clarity, only abietic acid was used as representative structure).

head.ce:title: Seeking brightness from nature: Sustainable carbon dots-based AIEgens with tunable emission wavelength from natural rosin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable carbon dots-based AIEgens with adjustable fluorescence emission wavelength prepared from rosin using hydrothermal methods.

Aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) have many applications, including bioimaging, organic light-emitting diodes, sensing and anti-cancer therapy. Most AIEgens, however, suffer from unsustainable synthesis. Here, we have developed carbon dots-based AIEgens (CD-AIEgens), with adjustable fluorescence wavelength, that can be prepared from sustainable rosin in a one-step hydrothermal process. The fluorescence wavelength of solutions containing as-prepared CD-AIEgens can be adjusted between 430 nm and 582 nm by varying the concentration. The adjustable emissive wavelength is attributed to inherent FRET between the carbon cores and aggregates of tricyclic phenanthrene moieties on the surface of the CD-AIEgens. Making use of their adjustable fluorescence, we successfully used the CD-AIEgens as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink for encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) was first proposed by Tang’s group in 2001 [1] and has since found use in many applications, including bioimaging [2–7], organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [8,9], sensing [10] and anti-cancer therapy [11–15]. Unlike aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) chromophores, AIE luminogens (AIEgens) show stronger emission when aggregated [16–18]. AIEgens are currently attracting much attention because of their excellent photophysical properties [19], including strong resistance to photobleaching [4,20] and high emission in the solid state [3,21]. Several types of AIEgen, including metal particles [22], organic molecules [3,10,16] and polymers [8,23], have been described. Interestingly, some of these AIEgens not only show adjustable emissive intensity, they also demonstrated tunable emissive wavelength by designing molecules using twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) strategy [24,25]. Nevertheless, most of these AIEgens require toxic precursors or complicated syntheses, which has hindered their practical application. Sustainable AIEgens with adjustable emissive wavelength would, therefore, represent a major advance over existing AIEgens.

Carbon dots, which are fluorescent nanodots that show stable photoluminescence, can be easily prepared from sustainable biomass [5,26–28]. Unfortunately, most of these carbon dots suffer from ACQ [9,29,30] because of strong π-π interactions in their graphitizing cores [9,31,32]. Here, we used rosin, a sustainable natural material from pine trees, and ethylenediamine (EDA) to prepare CD-AIEgens that do not suffer from ACQ (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared CD-AIEgens have a carbonized graphitic fluorescent core and their surface is decorated with non-planar tricyclic phenanthrene moieties (TPMs), derived from rosin, which prevent π-π interactions between the carbonized chromophore cores. In the non-aggregated state, the TPMs did not form clusters, which promoted non-radiative decay. On aggregation of the CD-AIEgens, however, varying clusters emerge at condensed solutions and solid aggregates, which are indispensable for the light emission. In such clusters, electrons are overlapped to afford extended delocalization and simultaneously rigidified conformation, thus leading to fluorescence [33]. The rationally designed CD-AIEgens demonstrated good photostability and solid fluorescence, which enabled their application as a color reversible water-resistant fluorescent ink.

Rosin (acid value 155–166 mg KOH/g) was purchased from Qifei Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). Ammonia (25%), tetraethyl orthosilicate, ethylenediamine, citric acid and anhydrous ethanol were all analytical grade reagents. Other solvents were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents were used as received, without further purification. Deionized water was generated using a Clever-Q30 UT ultrapure water system (Zhiang Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

Rosin (0.4 g) was dissolved in deionized water (70 mL) and mixed with ethylenediamine (1 mL). The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (volume 100 mL) and a hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 300 min, giving a yellow suspension. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble matter and the filtrate was freeze dried to provide a solid. The solid was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol with vigorous stirring for 20 min and then filtered. The filtrate was concentrated and vacuum dried to give CD-AIEgens as a deep yellow powder.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (0.6 mL) and ammonia (0.3 mL) were added dropwise to a stirred solution of CD-AIEgens in ethanol (8.0 mL). The mixture was then allowed to rest for 24 h to obtain the silica composite gel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of citric acid-based carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of citric acid (0.4 g) in deionized water (70 mL) was mixed with ethylenediamine (1 mL) and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (volume 100 mL). After hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 300 min, the brown solution was freeze dried to provide a solid product. The solid product was dissolved in deionized water to obtain a citric acid-based carbon dots ink.

Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded in DMSO‑d 6 using an AVANCE III HD 500 MHz spectrometer (Bruker Corporation, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, New York, NY, USA). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Fourier FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using an X'Pert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, UK). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1950 ultraviolet–visible spectrofluorometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Photoluminescence (PL) was measured using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Inc.). Photoluminescence quantum yield and fluorescence decay curves was measured using a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., Edinburgh, UK).

The CD-AIEgens were firstly characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 a). The TEM images showed that most of the CD-AIEgens have an average diameter of ~ 10 nm (Fig. 1b), with crystal distances of 0.21 nm and 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (102) crystallographic faces of graphite carbon (Fig. 1a inset) [34–37]. XRD spectra showed that the CD-AIEgens have a similar crystal structure to that of rosin, with an enhanced signal at 20–30° (Figure S1), which was attributed to the graphitized carbon core of the CD-AIEgens [35,38]. Analysis by XPS showed that the CD-AIEgens contain C (~85%), N (~5%) and O (~10%) (Fig. 1c). High resolution scans of the C, N and O elements of the CD-AIEgens were also obtained (Figure S2) [39–43]. Signals at 398.2 eV, 399.2 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.2 eV demonstrated that the CD-AIEgens contain pyridine N, N–H, pyrrole N and graphite N moieties (Figure S2) [40,44,45], and a signal at 531.6 eV demonstrated that the CD-AIEgens contain C = O moieties (Figure S2) [46]. The presence of a signal for N–H in the samples suggests that amide bonds were formed during the preparation, and this was confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum of the CD-AIEgens (Fig. 1d). Specifically, carbonyl moieties (–COOH) of natural rosin was assigned as the signal at 1690 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum (Figure S3). While, a signal of CD-AIEgens was observed at 1645 cm−1, which belongs to carbonyl moieties of amide. This result further confirmed that an amide bond has been formed during the hydrothermal process. The chemical structure of the CD-AIEgens was further investigated using 1H NMR spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectra of the CD-AIEgens and rosin are very similar. Specifically, signals of CD-AIEgens in the range of 0.52–2.31, 4.85–5.71 and 6.84–7.17 ppm well matched corresponding signals of natural rosin (Figure S4), which were assigned as –CH3, –CH2 and –CH of TPM moieties. This result confirmed that CD-AIEgens well inherited TPM moieties from natural rosin. Moreover, the OH signal of the carboxylic acid in rosin has been replaced by an amide NH signal (8.01 ppm) in the CD-AIEgens (Figure S4) [26], which was consistent with FT-IR analysis. This indicates that, in addition to a carbonized core, the CD-AIEgens are structurally similar to natural rosin. These results all demonstrate that the CD-AIEgens contain a carbonized core, with TPMs anchored onto the surface by amide linkages.

Having determined the structure of the CD-AIEgens, we next investigated their fluorescence. Excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm for fluorescence measurement since it was the best exciting wavelength (Figure S5). The CD-AIEgens were dissolved in ethanol, which is a good solvent, and water, which is a poor solvent, was then added to the ethanol solution. The fluorescence emission of the solution of CD-AIEgens increased continuously until the amount of water reached 70% (Fig. 2 a and Figure S6). The fluorescence lifetime increased from 1.63 ns to 1.81 ns upon addition of water (Fig. 2b), and the fluorescence quantum yield increased from 21.5% to 28.4%. This is because aggregation of the CD-AIEgens, caused by addition of water, promotes clustering of TPMs on the surface of the CD-AIEgens and facilitates fluorescence emission of the carbon cores [16,47,48]. This clustering-induced emission might be attributed to amide moieties and carbonyl moieties. These units formed through space conjugation in the clusters/aggregates, which promoted the fluorescence emission after clustering [49,50]. Addition of more than 70% water triggers precipitation of the CD-AIEgens, leading to reduced fluorescence intensity [5,32]. We also investigated the effect of temperature on fluorescence of the CD-AIEgens. Reversible fluorescence was observed over several cycles of heating and cooling (Fig. 2c and Figure S7), which can be attributed to temperature-regulated clustering of the TPMs. At higher temperatures, the clusters of TPMs deformed, which reduces radiative decay and fluorescence intensity whereas, at lower temperatures, clustering of the TPMs is promoted, leading to increased radiative decay and higher fluorescence intensity [5,51]. We investigated the effect of viscosity on the fluorescence of the CD-AIEgens by adding glycerol to tune the viscosity. Addition of increasing amounts of glycerol to an ethanol solution of CD-AIEgens produced a continuous enhancement in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2d and Figure S8) because clustering of the TPMs is greatly promoted in more viscous solutions [16]. Photostability of CD-AIEgens was also studied. CD-AIEgens did not show obvious fluorescence decrease upon UV irradiation for 260 min (Figure S9). Additionally, ultrasonic treatment did not obviously reduce the fluorescence emission of CD-AIEgens (Figure S10). All of these results demonstrate that CD-AIEgens have good fluorescence and that the intensity of emission can be tuned by both temperature and viscosity. The fluorescence of CD-AIEgens also showed nice response to cations. Specifically, various cations by adding the test ion (50 μM) to an ethanol solution with water fraction of 70% of CD-AIEgens (20 μg mL−1), and fluorescence quenching was caused by Cu2+ and fluorescence enhancement was triggered by Al3+ (Figure S11). This result suggests that CD-AIEgens can be employed for sensing cations in this polluted water.

Interestingly, as the concentration of CD-AIEgens in ethanol solution was increased from 0.01 mg mL−1 to 0.6 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity increased, without an obvious shift in wavelength (Fig. 3 a and Figure S12). This concentration-dependent fluorescence enhancement could be both caused by the effect of AIE and increased concentration. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis also showed the cluster size of CD-AIEgens increased when the concentration intensified (Figure S13), which further demonstrated clustering-induced emission partially contributed to the fluorescence enhancement. As the concentration was increased beyond 35 mg mL−1,however, the emission underwent a continuous red shift, from 430 nm to 582 nm (Fig. 3b). The CIE coordinates also showed a trend for the color of the solution to change from blue to yellow (Fig. 3c). Fluorescence emission ranging from blue to yellow can thus be obtained by varying the concentration of the CD-AIEgens, and doping CD-AIEgens into a silica composite gel at different concentrations produced fluorescence emission with different colors (Fig. 3d). We propose the following mechanism for this interesting fluorescence emission. Low concentrations (0.01–0.6 mg mL−1) of CD-AIEgens promote aggregation, which facilitates the clustering of TPMs and increases the fluorescence of the carbon cores, which is similar to the behavior of the fluorescence of CD-AIEgens in ethanol/water mixtures [52]. The possible mechanism for the red shift fluorescence at high concentration of CD-AIEgens was fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between these TPM aggregates (energy acceptors) and carbon cores (energy donors) occurs, leading to red-shifted fluorescence emission (Fig. 3e) [2]. This mechanism was also proposed by Chen’s and Liu’s group to explain the AIE emission of carbon dots [53]. Specifically, upon excitation, the carbon cores emit blue photons, which are absorbed by the TPM aggregates, which then produce emission at ~ 525 nm (Fig. 3e). To verify our proposed mechanism, we measured the fluorescence of natural rosin, which consists mainly of TPMs, at concentrations of 0.1–20 mg mL−1. As expected, solutions of natural rosin showed concentration-dependent fluorescence emission between 515 nm and 525 nm upon excitation at 450 nm (Figure S14), which is exactly the emission of the carbon cores, suggesting that our proposed mechanism is possibly correct.

Before exploring possible applications of the CD-AIEgens, we investigated their fluorescence in the powdered state. The CD-AIEgens showed yellow solid-state fluorescence upon UV irradiation (Figure S15). CD-AIEgens powder had a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 5.01%. As a comparison, the common ACQ CDs were synthesized according to reported methods from sodium alginate and citric acid, respectively [26,29]. These ACQ CDs had a low PLQY only with the value of 0.04% and 0.06%, respectively. The intensity of the fluorescence emission was only 0.4% lower, i.e. essentially unchanged, after excitation at 365 nm for 50 min (Figure S16), demonstrating that powdered CD-AIEgens are highly resistant to photobleaching. Their high photostability suggests that the CD-AIEgens are very suitable for specialized printing applications and we next explored their potential as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink for encryption. To be useful for this type of application, an ink must be water resistant so we next investigated the stability of CD-AIEgens printed onto paper. Paper with images of bamboo (printed with citric acid-based carbon dots) and a panda (printed with CD-AIEgens) was washed under running water (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescent image of the bamboo disappeared after washing for 1 min, while that of the panda was still visible after washing for 60 min, indicating the stable attachment of the CD-AIEgens to the paper (Fig. 4a). This stable interaction is likely attributable to hydrophobic interactions between the TPM coronas of the CD-AIEgens and the paper. After demonstrating the effectiveness of CD-AIEgens in printing applications, we printed patterns onto a filter paper using an ethanol solution of as-prepared CD-AIEgens. Under white light, it is almost colorless and shows a yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation (Figure S17). When images printed with CD-AIEgens are sprayed with ethanol, the fluorescence under 365 nm UV light turns blue (Fig. 4b and Video S1) and, if the images are then air-dried, the fluorescence returns to the original yellow color (Fig. 4b and Video S1). This behavior suggests that as-prepared CD-AIEgens can be used as a reversible two-switch-mode ink. A possible mechanism for the observed color changes in the ink is illustrated schematically in Fig. 4c. Ethanol dilutes the aggregated CD-AIEgens and the TPM aggregates decompose, preventing FRET energy transfer between the TPM aggregates and the carbon cores. As a result, the blue fluorescence emission of the carbon cores becomes visible. Since the ethanol-triggered change in fluorescence color is switchable, the CD-AIEgens can be used in a robust two-switch-mode luminescence ink, which has great potential in encryption.

In summary, we have developed CD-AIEgens with an adjustable fluorescence wavelength by a one-step hydrothermal process, starting with sustainable rosin. The fluorescence wavelength of solutions containing the CD-AIEgens could be varied between 430 nm and 582 nm by altering the concentration. The adjustable emission wavelength of the CD-AIEgens is attributed to dynamic FRET between the carbon cores and surface aggregates of TPMs, derived from rosin. The adjustable fluorescence emission of the CD-AIEgens allowed them to be successfully used as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink for encryption. It is likely that other biomass molecules that possess this type of moiety could also be used to prepare sustainable and easily prepared CD-AIEgens with adjustable fluorescence emission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31700502), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572020BB07) and the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2018QNRC001). The authors are grateful for the funding.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127457.
